title: Machina Arcana - Second Edition - Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rLZUcW0DBTM

Daniel (@dungeondive)'s long-form review of **Machina Arcana — Second Edition**, Uri Bilik's steampunk-Lovecraftian dungeon crawl set in an alien mountain fortress that Daniel cleanly characterises as *at the mountains of madness in steampunk dress*. The headline verdict, delivered in his own one-sentence summary: *a rewarding frustration*.

The rewarding half is the genuinely original system underneath. Stamina is the overriding currency — heroes and monsters spend it for everything from movement to abilities to closing doors — and every map tile is densely seeded with interactive spaces (event spots, trap levers, workbenches, treasure chests, recharge stations, exploding barrels, chapter beacons). Loot is split into four decks (peril, weapons/items, artifacts, consumables) you choose from on draw, and armour pieces with matching icons combine into *fashion-souls* sets that further accept augment slots. Combat is dirt-simple — roll dice, add pips, beat armour, one damage — which leaves all the depth in *how you spend stamina*. New diagonal-movement rules quietly open every tile up.

The frustrating half is the 2nd-edition rulebook. All the rules are there, but the writing is technical-writer dense, the terminology is locally non-standard (an attack is a *unit effect*; abilities are *owned* and *activate*), and the mechanisms are so deliberately intertwined that the prose can't easily untangle them on the page. Daniel recommends a community cheat sheet plus a second read after a play.

----

Daniel's verdict — *a rewarding frustration* — is one he reserves for a small set of games. Which game would *you* put under that exact label, and would you still recommend it to a stranger?
